exemplar.title: To what extent is feminism portrayed differently in 19th-century British literature written by male compared to female authors, and how does it compare to modern feminism?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is feminism portrayed differently in 19th-century British literature written by male compared to female authors, and how does it compare to modern feminism?

subject.title: English Lang & Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Language & Literature combines the study of language and literature, analyzing both literary and non-literary texts across various media and contexts. Students explore how language shapes meaning, identity, and culture through critical examination of diverse text types including advertisements, speeches, novels, and digital media. The course develops analytical skills, cultural understanding, and critical thinking about communication in different contexts and purposes.

strengths: Title and introduction communicate focus accurately and effectively.
Methodology section outlines database use and informed source selection.
Clear signposting of comparison section maintains reader orientation.
weaknesses: Occasional digressions into biographical detail reduce concision.
Research question covers two comparative axes and is thus rather broad.
Citation style inconsistency and lack of explicit analytical framework limit methodological effectiveness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work communicates its topic and purpose with clarity and focus (strand 5), maintains a consistent research question throughout (strand 4), and provides a mostly complete methodology (strand 4). Minor lapses in concision, some redundancy, and an overly broad question justify placing this criterion at the upper end of the 3â6 range but below perfect. A score of 5 appropriately reflects strong performance tempered by these areas for improvement.

strengths: Solid knowledge of the two novels and accurate plot summaries.
Integration of key secondary scholarship (e.g., Knight, Saxena) to support claims.
Generally accurate use of subject-specific terms like intersectionality and patriarchal ideology.
weaknesses: Reliance on non-scholarly sources (Wikipedia, BBC Bitesize) undermines academic rigour.
Terminology is at times imprecise or informally used (e.g., âunfeministâ, âportravalâ).
Some quotations serve general commentary rather than detailed textual analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates good knowledge of the topic and an effective, if somewhat inconsistent, use of terminology (band 3â4). While historical context and textual evidence are well handled, the inclusion of tertiary sources and occasional imprecision in terms keep performance out of the highest band. A score of 4 captures this solid but not consistently excellent performance.

strengths: A broad and relevant research base, including peer-reviewed journals, informs the investigation.
Thoughtful comparative analysis of character agency reflects engagement with feminist theory.
Conclusion synthesizes findings effectively, reinforcing the central argument.
weaknesses: Evaluation is fairly general with limited interrogation of methodological limitations or source biases.
Heavy reliance on tertiary sources (Britannica, BBC Bitesize) limits authority.
Analysis often summarizes plot rather than critically examining power dynamics; lacks counter-arguments and direct textual citations in places.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality is generally good and relevant (strand 5), analysis is thoughtful but uneven with mostly supported conclusions (strand 4), and argument development is solid though evaluation remains superficial (strand 4). These sub-scores in the 4â6 band suggest an overall Critical Thinking mark at the mid-range. A score of 8 balances the strengths of coherent argument and relevant research against the need for deeper critical evaluation and source rigor.

strengths: Structure meets all EE requirements with clear section headings and logical flow.
Bibliography is clearly labeled and alphabetized, aiding source traceability.
Section heading formatting aids reader orientation.
weaknesses: In-text citations switch inconsistently between parentheses and URLs; some long URLs break margins.
Page numbering is missing or not clearly visible.
Transitions between sections can be abrupt, and occasional typos undermine professionalism.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation follows the required EE format with appropriate structure (strand 3â4) and generally correct layout considerations (strand 3â4). However, inconsistent citation formatting, missing page numbers, and abrupt transitions prevent a top-band score. A mark of 3 reflects a mostly adequate presentation with room for refinement.

strandSelections.reasoning: A separate Methodology section explains the rationale for text selection, the application of modern feminist theory, and the use of databases such as JSTOR and EBSCO to locate sources.  The student cites a mixture of academic articles, theses and popular articles, showing some informed selection.  Nonetheless, citation style is inconsistent, evaluative comment on source reliability is limited, and no clear analytical framework (e.g., specific feminist concepts or close-reading method) is laid out.  This reflects a mostly complete but not fully effective methodology, aligning with the 3â4 band.

Headings are formatted consistently and the bibliography is alphabetised, but in-text citations switch between parenthetical references and bare URLs; page numbering is not visible, and some long URLs break the margin.  Overall layout considerations are present and mostly correct, yet imperfections lower the score to the bottom of the 3â4 band.

Roughly thirty sources are listed, including peer-reviewed journals (*BrontÃ« Studies*, *Dickens Quarterly*), books and encyclopaedia entries.  Most relate directly to feminism or the chosen novels, so the research base is largely relevant.  Nevertheless, reliance on popular webpages and patchy citation practice prevent the work from reaching the constant relevance and authority expected in the top band; a mid-band score of 5 is appropriate.

The student states the focusâ âModern Feminism in Classic Literature: An Analysis of Great Expectations and Jane Eyreâ âin both the title and introduction, and the opening paragraphs clearly explain why these two texts were selected and what aspect of feminism will be explored.  The essay maintains that focus throughout the body sections (separate analyses, comparison, conclusion).  Although occasional digressions into biographical detail are slightly lengthy, the reader is never in doubt about the central purpose.  Hence the work meets the descriptor for accurate and effective communication, but minor lapses in concision and occasional redundancy justify a score of 5 rather than 6.

A logical comparison section and a synthesising conclusion demonstrate argument development, but evaluation of findings is fairly general (âup to each reader and their cultural contextâ) and does not critically interrogate methodological limits or source biases.  The discussion therefore shows good but not fully critical engagement, meriting 4 within the 4â6 band.

The essay offers clear comparative pointsâe.g., Janeâs financial agency vs Estellaâs sexual powerâand links them to feminist theory, showing thoughtful analysis.  However, many claims remain descriptive (plot summary) or are asserted with limited close textual proof, and counter-arguments are seldom weighed.  Conclusions are âmostlyâ but not consistently supported, matching the lower end of the 4â6 band.

The essay follows the required EE structure (title page, abstract omitted but not mandatory under the new guide, table of contents, introduction, body, conclusion, bibliography).  Section headings are clear, and internal signposting supports the reader.  Minor issuesâoccasional abrupt transitions and uneven subsection lengthsâprevent a perfect score, but all structural requirements are met.

The student demonstrates solid knowledge of the two novels, summarising plot points accurately and referencing historical reception.  Secondary sources are integrated to support claims (e.g., Owsley 2013; Knight 2007); however, a number of non-scholarly or tertiary sources (Medium, StudyCorgi, Wikipedia) dilute academic rigour, and some quotations are used for general commentary rather than detailed textual evidence.  Overall the knowledge is good but not consistently excellent.

Key terms such as **intersectionality, patriarchal ideology, female autonomy, Victorian ideal woman** and **binary portrayal** are used mostly accurately, revealing understanding of feminist literary discourse.  Minor errors (âunfeministâ, âportravalâ) and occasional informal diction show that terminology is not always precise or consistent, keeping performance in the 3â4 band at a solid level.

The research question is explicitly stated and consistently referenced: *âTo what extent is feminism portrayed differently in 19th-century British literature written by male compared to female authors, and how does it compare to modern feminism?â*  It is clear and linked to the discussion, yet it contains two comparative axes (male vs female author; 19th-century vs modern feminism) which makes it rather broad for an EE of 4 000 words.  The analysis occasionally struggles to address the âmodern feminismâ component with depth, showing that the focus is only partially tight.  Therefore the strand sits comfortably in the 3â4 band, at the upper end.

annotations.comment: Terms like âunfeminineâ require theoretical definition; clarify with feminist concept definitions to avoid ambiguity.
Inclusion of diverse source types indicates breadth of research, showing intellectual engagement.
The title clearly communicates the focus on feminist analysis of two classic novels, establishing purpose and scope effectively.
Word count is clearly stated and within the maximum, demonstrating adherence to formatting requirements.
Table of contents is comprehensive, but page numbers could be more legibleâconsider repositioning or styling for clarity.
Page numbering is not visible in the footer; include clear page numbers to improve navigation.
Introduction provides strong historical context and rationale, situating the rise of feminist criticism effectively.
Reliance on tertiary sources like Britannica limits academic rigor; integrate peer-reviewed journal articles to strengthen evidence.
Methodology section outlines database use and source selection, demonstrating informed planning.
The methodology lacks a defined analytical framework (e.g., specific feminist concepts or close-reading steps); adding this would clarify approach.
Biographical detail on BrontÃ« is informative but would benefit from direct linkage to her feminist perspective in the novel.
Use of period reception (North British Review) effectively supports understanding of initial feminist interpretations.
Integration of the quote âI am no birdâ¦â provides solid textual evidence to support claims of autonomy.
Many paragraphs summarise plot rather than critically examining power dynamics; deepen analysis by connecting to feminist theory.
Transition between biographical discussion and novel analysis is abrupt; introduce signposting to improve flow.
Analysis lacks engagement with counter-arguments or alternative readings; integrating scholarly debates would strengthen evaluation.
Section heading for Dickens analysis is clearly formatted, aiding reader orientation.
Claims about Dickensâs unfeminist portrayals lack direct textual citations; include specific passages to substantiate assertions.
The analysis section would benefit from direct quotes from Great Expectations to illustrate female character construction.
Reliance on BBC Bitesize and StudyCorgi reduces academic authority; replace with peer-reviewed scholarship.
Engagement with Knightâs and Saxenaâs arguments demonstrates effective integration of secondary scholarship.
Comparison section is clearly signposted, aligning reader expectations with analytical focus.
Typo âportravalâ appears in the comparison paragraph; proofread for spelling to enhance professionalism.
The discussion of realism and autonomy is nuanced, reflecting thoughtful critical engagement with source material.
Comparison could more explicitly map findings back to each axis of the research question to tighten focus.
Conclusion synthesizes findings well, reinforcing key comparisons and aligning with the thesis.
Conclusion contains repetitive phrasing; refine language for conciseness and avoid redundancy.
Bibliography is clearly labeled, ensuring that readers can locate source list easily.
Citation styles are inconsistent and rely heavily on non-scholarly sources; standardize formatting and prioritise peer-reviewed work.
An orphan URL appears outside the bibliography list; integrate or remove for cleaner presentation.
Reference to Wikipedia may undermine academic credibility; replace with scholarly sources for factual background.
Poetry Foundation entry offers general biographical detail but does not inform feminist analysis; consider more relevant critical sources.
Use of a specialized longreads article enhances engagement with alternative scholarly perspectives.
Missing page numbering in footer reduces usability; ensure all pages include visible numbers.
